speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] or-- or English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think once or twice I've been asked if I was South African. [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE] Read that out.

speaker: interviewer
text: To read-- read what's written on here? OK. March 29, 1995. Gershon Wilkenfeld is the survivor, and the interviewer is Dasha Gilden. The interview is held in Sydney, Australia, and in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before you start, what--

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Dasha Gilden, D-A-S-H-A, G-I-L-D-E-N. I'm-- today is-- today is the 29th of March, 1995, and I'm conducting an interview with Gershon Wilkenfeld, and we're in Sydney, Australia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hi. If you could please tell me your name, and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My name is Gerhard Wilkenfeld, known as Gershon. Name is spelled--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gerhard--

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-R-H-A-R-D W-I-L-K-E-N-F-E-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my name given to me at birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever had any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I have a Hebrew name, which was given to me, and that is Elyakim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-L-Y-A-K-I-M, spelled in English, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been known by any other nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I haven't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 4th of August, 1926, in Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are your parents from? What are your parents' names and where are they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came from Poland, or were born in Poland, in the Cracow area. I won't go into the little names, little religious--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Gittel, or Gitla.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-I-T-T-E-L. And my father was Isidor Leib Leo-- Leib being [INAUDIBLE], the Hebrew name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-I-B. It's-- it's really a translation from the Hebrew of Leo being [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know your mother's family name that she was born with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's family name was Wein, W-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where-- where did you grow up? Did you grow up in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in Berlin, like I said before. At the age of two, the family decided to leave, and we moved to a small town in the center of Germany called Aken, spelled A-K-E-N. And it's Aken Elbe, in brackets, because it was situated on the River Elbe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I believe there is another Aken in a different place, and then there's another one that's called Aken on the Belgian border. So it's usually pronounced Aken Elbe. OK. Is that-- what's the nearest large-- larger town, well-known town, to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aken-- the nearest larger town, and the town that we, or the city that we belonged to as far as our communal affairs and Jewish identity refers to was a place called Dessau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-E-S-S-A-U. Dessau-Anhalt, which is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anhalt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anhalt, which is the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: The region.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or the region, as it was known. We were known for one thing, that Moses Mendelssohn was born in-- in Dessau. So it's mentioned in many history-- Jewish history books and so forth. In fact, I used to go to cheder in the little room that he was born in. But that's just by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's-- that's important. Can you just, for the tape's sake, explain what cheder is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, cheder is a little school. Cheder means-- actually, in Hebrew, it's a room. And it was a little room. But it's where, after-- in our case, after normal school hours, we were taught Hebrew reading, history, and all the things that go with Jewish education.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How far would Dessau be from Aken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dessau was about 17 kilometers from-- from Aken. There was a bus service. The usual thing was to ride by bike to get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your family move to Aken from Berlin originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I would imagine it had to do with economic circumstances. We had no family there. In fact, we were the only Jewish family in Aken. My father, who had come from Poland without any means, had to start business somewhere. And I don't know the exact reason why they picked on Aken, but I'm sure there was something that drew him there. And he started in business there quite successfully [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us now what business he was in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the business he went into was-- well, it's called different things in different countries. But it was mainly a time payment business. I should explain that Aken, being a small town, was surrounded by lots of farms, and-- with their workers attached to those farms. They were working in rather poor conditions, and needed goods, but they never had the money to pay for it. Now, this is a long time before there were credit cards and things like that. And he started off giving the goods to these people on time payment. So the thing went that he would take orders. He had a little warehouse. He would deliver the goods to them. And they would then-- then he would make the rounds from there, and collect his moneys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just to also say what sort of goods was it? Food? Was it actual goods from use?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it ranged from furniture to ladies dresses and underwear. Whatever the people required, he would buy. And some things he would stock. Others he would get in for them. But the main thing was, it was a time payment business which then would take him during the week to those various farms, places, villages. And the good for him, of course, was that he could regulate the business as far as that goes, that he didn't have to keep an open store. Being very religious, he could close it down on the weekend, on Saturday, and he didn't have to travel on that day. And that sort of fitted in very well in with his beliefs.

speaker: interviewer
text: He traveled every day? Did he actually stay away overnight?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He would go out, and come back and go-- you know, he had fixed runs that he would go to. But, of course, Friday afternoon was [HEBREW]. You have to receive the Sabbath, translated into English. And he didn't have to open his store on the Sabbath. And that was a big thing for him, or on holidays, of course, the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were an orthodox family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Orthodox Jewish family, although you lived in a town--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, although we lived away from it, what-- we belonged to the Dessau congregation, and on holidays, we would go there. We would stay overnight with other family, or hotel, or whatever, and would partake in the services at that congregation. We were part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that every Shabbat, every Saturday? Or did you do other practices-- religious practices at home with your own family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, we had our religious practices at home. It wasn't very easy. Many times, particularly when it came to kosher food, which had to be got from the larger towns. It wasn't that you could go down to the kosher butcher shop. And to this effect, my father actually went and did a course in-- in-- and became a shochet so that he could you know, do-- get his chooks from the farms, and kill his own chicken to conform with the kashrut laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, can you explain what a shochet is? Briefly. Briefly.

speaker: interviewee
text: A shochet is a person that knows how to ritually-- I don't know whether I can put that properly-- comply with the ritual laws that-- how animals should be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: To make them kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I won't go into details, yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, tell me a little bit more about your family. There was your father, and your mother, and yourself. Was there any other children in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I said before, I was two years old when we arrived there. Soon after, a sister was born to-- well, a daughter was born to the family, which was my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in 1928, in Dessau, where the hospital was. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was two years younger. I was born in '26.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, at a later date, in 1935, another boy was born, my brother, who was Norbert Nissan. And, unfortunately, both of them perished later on in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was our family, as it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your immediate family.

speaker: interviewee
text: The immediate family that we had. We had-- in Dessau, on the other hand, we had some relatives, in the sense that there weren't brothers, or sisters, or cousins, but they were slightly more distant. They had come from the same area in Poland where my parents came from, and in fact, like I said, they were second or third cousins. And we were sort of visiting them quite regularly. The fact was that I stayed with them on a regular basis on holidays, when, even though my parents might not be there, I would stay there overnight, and, you know, thus enable me to go to shul more often, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any other family at all, like grandparents? Did your parents have grandparents-- their own parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My-- there was a grandmother. My grandfather died, both sides. I never knew them. My grandmother was alive, but she passed away in 1937. But I only knew her from visiting Poland. There was a time in the early '30s where my father or my mother would go back on an annual basis to Poland to their birthplace where they had lots of family, and I would go with them. And that way, I got to know a lot of the places. I know the buildings they lived in, what the place looked like. I partook in their life, if you like. I can still recall it today exactly, you know, seeing [INAUDIBLE] or all the different festivals. But it was visits only. And like I said, this ceased in 1936 or '37. That become-- that was the end of it, as far as visiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: The end of going to Polish visits. Of course. Of course. As far as other religious practices, Jewish practices in your life-- for example, when your brother was born, naturally, you would have had a bris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: A circumcision ceremony. Where was that performed? Was that performed in your home, in the hospital, in Dessau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I must be honest. I cannot remember exactly. I suspect it would have been at the hospital in Dessau, because that's where the congregation was, and it was a very up to date hospital in those days. But I don't recall the-- the actual happenings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ritual OK. As far as having Pesach, Rosh Hashanah, you went to your relatives in Dessau and you were all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in most cases, yes. But it was very difficult because, you know, there were other times where we couldn't go, and we would just say our prayers at home. I mean, my father was very conversant with all these things, and-- it was on off, you know. One day-- one year yes, one year no. And we're only talking about a number of short years anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke German, obviously. I had a good understanding of Yiddish, which is what my parents spoke, and was spoken amongst friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish, they spoke amongst themselves, but mainly when they didn't want us to understand what they were saying. So it was German, mainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you speak any Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't speak Polish now either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I do speak Yiddish, and I do speak-- I speak German, of course, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started in Aken, in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what they called the Volkschule, the-- what's the first?

speaker: interviewer
text: Primary school.

speaker: interviewee
text: The primary school. At the age of six. I went there for the first few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year that would be--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not quite sure--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would that have been in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was in about '32 or '33. And I went to that school until 1937. And I was about to go into what they call the-- the [GERMAN], or, you know, the next type of schooling. I'm not sure how you'll relate this to the Australian system.

speaker: interviewer
text: They have a different system. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, by that time things had become already very difficult with regards to the politics of the German [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] machinery, the-- the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: The system.

speaker: interviewee
text: The system. You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that in a minute, as far as the government.

speaker: interviewee
text: The government, that's-- I'm looking for the words. I'm getting my words mixed up now.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. That's not a problem. Were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: The main thing was that whilst I was in school, and whilst I in Aken, I was very much involved-- my friends were non-Jewish kids. You know, we used to play together. We used to go hiking together and all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you dress the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we dressed the same, except that, of course, when they started to join the Hitler Youth, that's where the differences became noticeable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cover your head, though, when you were-- did you have your head covered?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no I wasn't-- I don't want to give you the impression that I was-- we were religious in a traditional sense. But my father did not wear a beard, or-- or this type of thing. You know? I think it was a different understanding in orthodoxy in-- in Germany in those days to what it is today.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a practicing Jew, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a practicing Jew, that's right, keeping Shabbat and all that, but not going with the whole Polish traditions of-- of ear locks and beard, and-- you know? I guess, in our religion, everybody has our own ways of saying, this is how much I keep, and this one I don't keep, you know? But I was coming back to this. I was very friendly with the boys in the street, and we used to go here and there. And that went on fine until I was to go into this higher school. And by then, of course, things have got worse, in 1937. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first time you actually felt that you were different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is what I'm coming to. That's when things started to become obvious to me, things like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] teachers singling me out, saying, you didn't do this right, you didn't do that right, and all this sort of thing. Other things--

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that clearly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the first episode of that? Do you remember a first episode where you felt it was because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the one that really-- besides little things-- that really sticks in my mind was that-- and I mentioned that before, that Aken is on the Elbe, which is a very long, big river. There was a swimming pool there. And as part of the schooling, seven children our age were meant to be taught swimming. It's not like it is today, that every second house has a swimming pool. That was the only facility there. And I remember going there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how old you were at that point? Do you remember approximately how old you were at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- you were 11. Oh, you remember that already being '37.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm talking about '37. I was born '26, if my arithmetic is right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] So-- but it's something that's stayed with me my whole life, is that when my turn came, and the teacher was to look after me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he actually got hold of me, and dunked me under the water, and forcibly tried to keep me under. That was no way of trying to teach me how to swim. He really wanted to cause me harm. Now, I don't know how far he would have gone or what he would have did. But all I know is that I managed to get out of his grip, and managed to come up, spewing water and all this sort of thing, and then running home. But to me, that was the first incident, really something that said to me, hey, you're being treated-- you're being singled out for bad treatment. [SIGHS] [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your regular teacher, regular class teacher? Or was that a special swimming teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a sports teacher who I had no other dealings with. But everybody knew that I was a Jew. I mean, I wasn't part of the youth movement-- you know, the Hitler Youth. I wasn't this. I wasn't allowed to go here. And by that-- in a small town, these things get-- it gets around very quickly. On top of it, I think that was also a certain knowledge that my father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had done reasonably well. He was a guy in the town that had a motor car, and I was the son, all that. You know, and you have all these things that go with it. But that was my first incident of really noticing this. And, of course, from then on, I had to look out, And I was very much aware of the situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other further contact with that particular teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and you might ask me, did I complain about it? I didn't, because it was quite obvious that I wasn't going to get anywhere with that. That wasn't the democracy that was in place at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other children were with the Hitler Youth-- all the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I can't tell you all of them. But, I mean, all my friends were, without exception.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside of school hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, they used to go to camps. They used to go here. They used to go skiing. They used to-- you know, it was a huge movement. You know, I wasn't-- I wasn't the only one. But it was very enthusiastically embraced, and it was giving them a reason to be happy with and happy for. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were aware of that? You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did it mean to you? I mean, as a kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was just-- at the time, you know, as a child, you feel, oh, I wish I could-- why am I not part of it? You know, I mean, one-- as a child, doesn't-- I mean, I remember thinking that to myself. Why am I-- what's the difference? What-- what have I done, or what have we done, sort of thing? But I had no answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember thinking that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember thinking that. But I had no answers for it, of course. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak to other kids about it, or to your family about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. I do not remember. I'm not saying that I didn't, but I-- I know there was one young boy that lived next door who I did speak-- you know, I was fairly close to. Unfortunately, I don't even remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he sort of, I think, took pity on me or whatever it was. And he used to play with me. But then his parents said to him, listen, don't play-- you know. He's a Jude, and, you know, you'll get into trouble, and all this. But I saw him every now and again, you know? His parents said that to him. He told you that. And did he live right near you and your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was in the house next door.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you used to go to his parents' house before all this happened? Did he used to come to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- it was a different times. People were not going from one place to another. People-- the Kids used to play in the streets. I mean, there were a few motor cars. I can show you in the photo, the street, what it looked like, if you want to see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will do that later. We'd like to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, people used to play. And I believe it was the same thing here. We're talking 50 years ago. People-- Kids used to play out in the street, and that's where you met other kids. And you used to play soccer, and you'd play this and that. And, of course, we had other playgrounds that we used to go to. And, actually, there was another instance but I don't think I'll go into this. We had a lake there that used to get frozen over. And one of the kids would try to push me into one of those holes at one stage. But it just came back to me right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, please talk about it if you feel it was an active antisemitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lake called [NON-ENGLISH], and it was a lake that used to freeze over in the wintertime. And we used to go skating on that lake. But what others used to do is they used to make holes in it to get fish out of there. And I thought-- I'm not sure whether, you know, how intentional it was, but-- it was intentional. But, I mean, how [INAUDIBLE] or if it was just a prank or all that. But kids try to shoved me down one of those holes at one stage, but I got away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about-- this was the beginning of '38, like, in that winter. But I had forgotten all about that. It just came back to me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But it came back to you as--

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole thing was that we knew we had to be on the lookout all the time. And-- but, of course, you can ask me is, well, why did you stay there? And I can't answer that. I haven't got the, you know, the-- if I would have been rational, if I would've been older, and later on I did get to that, I would have said, well, let's get out of here. But I suppose economic situations, all that. I can't-- I can't tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Everybody had different reasons. With your close friends, like that boy in the next building or anything, did you actually discuss with him that he wasn't allowed to play with you as much?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had no long discussions, no, no, no. You know, I mean, we concerned ourselves with the moment of what we were going to do, or what we did the day before, and all this sort of thing. There were no political discussions, or, you know, elaborate discussions with those boys. He would tell me what they did at camp, and different things, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a member of the Hitler Youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he would tell you what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, he'd sort of tell me, we did this. We did that, you know, and I would listen to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he mention any activities against Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No. He didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else that you can remember from those times of Hitler's rising, some memorable things that were happening affecting your life greatly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember other occasions. And I remember the rise of-- of the Hitler regime. One of the reasons being that Dessau was a city where the Junkers factory was. Junkers was the factory that manufactured a very well-known brand of airplanes. You might have-- you may remember there was a plane called Stuka, which is, in German, [GERMAN] bomber. That's a dive bomber.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember Hitler arriving there one day to initiate some-- some new plane or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Dessau?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Dessau, and seeing that whole parade of people, and seeing him standing in his--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Dessau at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there, yes. I used to go there quite regularly. And, of course, I didn't say before. I used to go regularly to Dessau to cheder anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you did say.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was a weekly or biweekly situation. I used to get on my bike and ride the 15 kilometers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the 15 kilometers on your bike take you, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends how I went, and it depends on the of the year. Sometimes it might take me half an hour, particularly if I got hold of a bus and hung on to the back of it. Other times, if it was snowing, and-- you had walk part of the time, so-- you know, but it might have taken an hour or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you went with your father-- you mentioned you had a car-- before Shabbat or before a holiday, how long did it take then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was an [INAUDIBLE] of 20 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the general size of the Jewish community in Dessau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure of the numbers, but it was a very wealthy congregation, money left to it by some previous congregants. And-- but I can't give you the numbers. But it was-- like I said, it had a beautiful synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- did it have a special name, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it was-- it was just the one big synagogue, which took up a whole sort of city block, and consisted of not only the ordinary synagogue. There was a small synagogue which was used during ordinary week prayers, when, you know, they couldn't fill the main hall. Attached to it was a building with social halls for weddings, and bar mitzvah, and these sort of things. Then there was a residence of the rabbi attached to it. And I think one of the teachers lived there as well. And the whole thing was built round-- on this block with a big center court where there were gardens inside. And that was also very good, because on high holidays, when people-- when there was a break in the services or that, they didn't have to spill out into the street like they do here sometimes, and maybe annoy neighbors or this sort of thing. They had the center court. And children could play there, and there was no fear of them coming in harm's way, and all that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll be doing a tape change.
